speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four with Israel Kipen. Israel, we were talking about the rise of Hitler's power. Was there a change in the relationship between the Jews and the Poles in Bialystok before the outbreak of war at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find it hard to give you an answer on that one for a very simple reason-- because the life in Bialystok-- as indeed in most Jewish towns-- was so integrated in terms of the Jewish self-- self-management in-- in-- on every level, whether it is in-- in-- in social or in-- or-- or in economic or in-- in public or whatever it is that there was hardly-- there was hardly any-- any contact between the Polish population and the Jewish population. In the smaller towns where-- where-- the-- where the--the-- the immediate physical contact and the economic interaction was much more prevalent-- because in the little townships where they lived, the-- the-- the middlemen of-- in agrarian products or-- or in building roads or things like that, they had direct contact with the-- with-- with the peasant trade. But in the big cities, especially in times like that, you-- you didn't-- you didn't see a-- a-- a-- a--an-- a-- a Polish person, really. You had to go to a certain definite place where you know you would see a Polish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then how-- well, how would you describe the first signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? I am not quite clear about this question.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. In terms of the imminence of war, what were the first signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the first real signs of danger of-- of-- of the oncoming tragedy was the Stalin-- the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact. The minute that pact was signed, there was no question anymore that-- that war is coming, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain the nature of this pact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Because the issue was at that-- at that stage-- at that stage, everybody knew that-- that-- that Hitler is ready to strike. But he was afraid. He was afraid that whichever way he will go, he doesn't want to have to fight on two fronts. No-- no politician, no general is-- is prepared for that. Now there is an exception. Alexander the Great did it willingly, but that's why he was Alexander the Great. But-- but otherwise, people want to have one front and not to have-- to be challenged from the back. And that was the nature of the agreement The-- the Ribbentrop-Molotov Agreement secured-- secured for Germany a one-front situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the pact signed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pack was signed in early '39. And once that was signed, everybody knew war is coming. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you became aware that war had broken out or that Germany had invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. It was the 1st of September. We were home. And at 9 o'clock, suddenly we had aeroplanes all over the city. And they came from a westerly direction at an angle going towards the railways-- railway yards. And we came down, of course. We went out of the-- into the yard, and we heard explosions. We knew that war has started. And that first feeling of looking up and seeing-- seeing bombs falling out from the aeroplanes and-- and saying which one is mine is the first encounter. That was-- that was because the war started officially at 4 o'clock in the morning when they-- they crossed the border. And whilst it took them three weeks to-- to-- to occupy Poland, the aeroplanes had no limits, of course, and they were everywhere at the same time. So by the-- by the-- the first-- the first purpose of any military action is to disrupt communication of the enemy. Yeah. If you disrupt the-- the-- the-- the railway lines and you can't send supplies, so you can't send reinforcements, and you can't send-- you have-- you have-- you have partly achieved your-- your-- your military purpose. And that was-- But I have a weak area. I came from-- from the countryside on another of those missions of collecting-- of collecting [INAUDIBLE] and so on. And on the way as I inched my way towards-- towards Bialystok, at every station I saw people farewelling their sons at the station, already called up for military service. And so that was about a week--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Polish ones. And by the time that I came down in Bialystok, I saw detachments of people with spades walking in the streets to start digging air-raid shelters. There was no question about it anymore. Everybody knew. Everybody knew that war is-- is-- in fact, only a-- a question of days or hours away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the immediate changes that took place after the-- this particular night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. The immediate changes that took place were absolutely immediate because, first of all, certain things had to be done in terms of internal arrangement. Blackouts was the first thing. There-- there was-- there was a civil-- civil organization guard, and people were appointed to-- to-- to keep-- keep guarded the streets to make sure that no light is shown in-- at-- at-- at nighttime and-- and to draw people's attention that their curtains are not fully drawn or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- young people in the-- in the-- of the population. They organized--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure. I was one of them. I was one of them standing in the night-- in the night chilly air doing this sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the-- the-- the flow of refugees started coming from the west, because no sooner did they cross the border the first people to harm are the Jews, yes? Because they were the first potential victims. So the only way to go is eastward. They came from the west eastwards. And in no time Bialystok, of a set population of 100,000 people, had the population of a quarter of a million to 300,000 people-- of refugees coming en masse with families, running away from the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a-- a tragic sight. People were in the streets, in the-- in the-- in the-- on the pavements. And I remember that in our home mother and grandmother were in the kitchen the whole day long cooking-- cooking meals. And we used to bring people from the streets to give them a meal, give them a hot meal a day, because they were worn out and hungry and-- and-- and-- and-- and no-- no-- no sleep. And-- and every night we used to give up our beds, the children used to give our beds, to bring people in to give them a night's sleep. And ultimately we had two families and we give-- to each of them we gave a room of their own. One was a rabbi and his wife and daughter, and another one was a family from Lodz, I think, also with a wife and a daughter. [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember even the name of the other family. And so they have taken a permanent stay with us to be fed and housed fully. And every night and every day it was coming and going all the time. People came-- strangers-- and we used to feed them. We used to do what we could for them. But-- and then there was not enough bread. Not only was not enough bread in terms of-- of flour, there was not enough facilities in a city of 100,000 people to bake for 300,000 people, in pure technical terms. So we used to get up-- I used to get up at 3:00 in the morning-- up at 3:00 in the morning, go to the other end of town where there was the bakery, and stand in a queue, and at 7 o'clock in the morning come home with a whole-- with a loaf of bread-- round, like, loaf of bread. And that was a major achievement, day in and day out, that loaf of bread. Those were the immediate impacts of-- of that. And-- and of course, as long as it doesn't touch you, you think that you are charitable and you are in a different category. The others were the-- the refugees. You were in your own home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family continue to go to synagogue in this immediate--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. That had nothing to do-- as long as-- as life in terms of-- of the internal was going on, what was between man and man and between man and God were two separate things. They are-- you know, the believing man has got-- does not exchange values. They are not-- they are not equating it. There are no exchanges. There are no-- no quid pro quo.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it change, again? When did the situation alter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed firstly-- firstly changed when the Russians crossed the border in-- I called it the Agreement of-- the Molotov Agreement and started occupying their part of Poland which was allotted to them by that agreement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had the Germans at this point--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: --marched into Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They have taken Bialystok. They've taken it in a orderly, exemplary fashion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did they walk into Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been the 21st, the 22nd of-- of September. Took three weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the immediate--

speaker: interviewee
text: --if not earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: --results of them walking in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The immediate results of that takeover is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or effects.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of-- of-- of that occupation is extraordinarily worth recalling. Within 24 hours of occupying Bialystok, the Chamber of Manufacturers, on instructions from the military-- the German military-- called all members of-- of the-- of the Chamber to a meeting. And a German officer addressed the meeting and said to the people, you are instructed-- you are ordered-- to open up your factories immediately. You will continue with your production. You will be provided with the raw material necessary for the type of goods that the German army requires. The bank-- the national bank-- will be instrumental in ensuring that you have got the cash flow to pay the wages to the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so it was. There was no question of if or what. The next day everything went back to-- to the status quo because it wasn't a question of selling the goods. The-- the-- the-- the buyer was there, they want, and that was the sort of product that they needed, military uniforms. That was a top product that Bialystok produced.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was it. And-- but-- but the orderliness of it was-- was overwhelming, and I remember. And I'm writing about it in my book in more detail because it contrasted so much with what happened a little while later when the Russians finally reached Bialystok from-- from the eastern side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a-- a powwow between them and the Germans at to retreat because they were in Bialystok not according to the agreement. According to that agreement, Bialystok belonged to the Russians, not to the Germans. They-- they tried to put the Russians before an accomplished situation, but the Russians insisted on their rights according to the agreement. And the way they arrived, in the same manner they've disappeared-- without any impact, orderly, out. And then suddenly the first detachment of the Russians arrived. And the contrast of the two armies was something that-- that you had to see to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you share that with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] First of all, the Germans were well-fed, beautifully dressed. They were victors, their heads high. There was no problem about their behavior. The same streets in which the Polish officers and soldiers used to walk on a Sunday-- the outings were the social life of the-- of the-- of the people took place in the normal way of interaction in-- in those days-- instead of Polish soldiers, German soldiers and German officers were walking. But there was no-- no friction. There was no visible or-- or, as far as I know, any-- any form of-- of aggression and so on. They were very well-behaved. Nothing has happened as far as as the army's behavior was in the first days of their arrival. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians-- the Russians came. They came. The first thing they did when they arrived? They went to the jails, opened the jails, and let all-- all the jailed people out. That was the first act because in the jail there were plenty of communists, of course. So the first act, together with the communist, all the criminals got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then started-- started the process of nationalization, so to speak. Every manufacturer was in turn visited and-- and-- and the organization in each factory, a nucleus of workers which organized itself as-- as a subunit of the party to-- serving the new masters and the-- and the-- and the communist state. They tried in their own way, in their own unprepared and unrehearsed way, to become public servants and-- and political activists and so on. And-- and I remember seeing very clearly-- well, the factory-- we were told, you run the factory, but it's not yours anymore. And then one day a Russian civilian came to our home, together with the chairman of the committee-- one of our workers that took over the factory. We were not-- we were there. We had to go there to-- to-- to formally run it, but we were not the owners anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: --there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we-- we go-- we went to the factory to supervise to make sure that the state is getting everything that they are entitled to, yeah, of nationalized property. And they-- and then they came to our house and they took an inventory of all the furniture and everything that was in the house, written up. They came to the-- to the sideboard. In the old-- in those days, they were two-storied sideboards with glass-inlaid windows in the-- in the-- in the doors and so on. He looked at it and didn't know what it was. Coming from Russia, he hasn't seen it. He-- he didn't know what it was, and he-- and-- and he wrote down that it is a wardrobe. He just didn't know what it was. And he wrote it all down, and then he turned round to us and said to us, you can [? live ?] here, but remember, this furniture is not yours anymore. It belongs to the state. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that worker out of [INAUDIBLE] signed the protocol, and that was the end of-- of that story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the money situation change?

speaker: interviewee
text: The money situation with the arrival of the Russians was momentarily rather extraordinary, as I mentioned before, because suddenly with the-- everybody knew that in Russia there is a surfeit of money and a shortage of goods. So as it is generally in wartime, you know, there is a story-- there is a joke being said about the difference between war and peace-- that in peacetime you keep the money in the safe and the goods on the shelf, in wartime you keep the money on the shelf and the goods in the safe. So that-- that is exactly how it was. People bought everything because suddenly everybody had money suddenly. And they bought things so irrationally, so absurdly, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Such as? My own uncle, the clever, intelligent, brilliant man, brilliant man-- you would give him-- you would tell him, 3,745 and 3/4 yards of material at 3 and 3/4 kopeks. How much would it be? He would write it down. This sort of a brain. This man ran through whatever savings he had and bought plywood from this man. Plywood. In wartime, plywood. You can't lift a single piece of plywood. It's huge like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you have to run tomorrow, if you have to-- to-- to escape, what will you do with plywood? If you buy a-- a-- a-- a ring that has got a diamond in it, all right. You can sew it in into your coat and-- and go with it, around with it, and there is a chance that you may hold on to it. They came to us to buy our [INAUDIBLE], which was huge packets of-- of fluffy brushed material that they used to put inside the-- between the-- the-- the lining and the outer clothes of the-- for warmth. Each-- each piece of-- was-- was 10 times as big as that rug. What are you going to do with it? It needs-- in the-- in the-- where will you keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet, whatever we produced was sold. It was all sold. It was sold. Every day they used to bring Polish money and Russian money, and it was-- it was totally absurd. Totally absurd. There was no rhyme or reason to the human behavior.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and the people just were-- it's mass psychosis, and that's how it-- and of course when the crunch came, they didn't run anyhow. But-- but-- but if you would have stopped for one second to think, what are you doing? It's-- hold on to your money, no matter how little it is. At least maybe it's worth something. But not-- not to buy immovable objects. And that's what was done. To such an extent the irrationality replaced rational thinking, rational human behavior, because suddenly the-- the-- the normal pattern of-- of-- of logic is giving way to a-- to a sort of-- of-- of perplexity that-- that has got no-- no beginning and no end and no rationale and no-- no logic in it whatsoever, and-- and suddenly the most logical, intelligent person behaves like a-- like-- like a senseless child. How long did this situation continue unchallenged?

speaker: interviewee
text: It continued-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a while. I can only say-- give-- give testimony to it because I left. I left in the 31st of December, 1939. That was my-- my New Year's outing, crossing the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, if I could just take it back before you left. Were there any restrictions made on you as Jews by the Russians at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in terms of-- of social stratification. Not otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the nature--

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --but the only-- the only restrictions that were made were that in Hebrew Gymnasium, which was my alma mater, the educational-- the new educational authorities dictated to the college that Hebrew is prohibited because Hebrew was-- was the expression of Zionist a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] point of view. And Hebrew had to be replaced with Yiddish. And Yiddish was the-- the accepted common tongue of the-- the-- the-- the Russian [NON-ENGLISH]. They had their own Yiddish written in-- in a-- in-- in a way where Hebrew words were written with-- with Yiddish spellings. So if you take today a book published by the [NON-ENGLISH] in-- of-- from-- from-- in-- from Russia, you find it very hard to read it without just raising an eyebrow every so often about the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- for the-- for the trained eye to accept it. But that's how it was. But that was a dictate of a political nature. How it translated itself in terms of the self-govern-- governing bodies of the community of the kehilla, I'm not in a situation to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the-- the-- but if-- if that is any indication at all, I think it is saying a great deal because it goes to the very core about some of the basic elements of the transformation as far as Jewish lives are concerned. The word 'Zionism' became suddenly traif. It was-- it was unmentionable, and-- and-- and-- and that was it. Klematynowski, the man whom I saved, the-- the leader of the k-- told me when I sat in his very spacious cabinet that when he goes to-- when he goes now to-- to the-- to the locales where he was prominent only a short few weeks ago and everybody sought his friendship and his advice, suddenly, he said, everybody's shunning him. Nobody's coming near him. Nobody wants to have anything to do with him because he's-- he's-- he's a-- a Jewish Zionist leader. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever put in a position where you had to hide in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not really. Not really. Not really. Well, there were such momentary-- momentary things so that, you know, parents want to protect their children in the last minute, but I was not doing the actual activities. I was not called into the army because I was not quite 21. By the time that they-- they looked for younger people, it was all over, and-- and I was not really in. But-- but the whole thing took three weeks. The-- the whole campaign took three weeks. Poland fell to pieces in three weeks. 1/3 of it was taken over by Russians, 2/3 taken by the Germans, so that was it. It was just carved up and cut-and-dried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Israel. That's the end of tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tape four.
